title: Melting moments
subtitle: Crumbly, buttery biscuits with a cornflour crumb
excerpt: Melting moments are round, crumbly biscuits with a very short texture, made from butter, cornflour, and a little flour, rolled in desiccated coconut and baked until pale. The name is precise: the biscuit dissolves on the tongue rather than snapping or crunching. They are a staple of Australian and British home baking.
sourceNotes: Melting moments are documented in Australian and British home baking from the mid-twentieth century onward. The formula is standard: softened butter, icing sugar, cornflour, and a little plain flour. The high cornflour content is what gives the characteristic crumble. Firmly public domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked biscuits in a single layer. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; low temperature keeps the biscuits pale

content.text: Melting moments are small, pale biscuits made from butter, icing sugar, and a lot of cornflour. The cornflour is the whole point: it makes the biscuit short and crumbly, dissolving on the tongue rather than snapping. Rolled in coconut before baking, they come out round and very pale.

Beat the butter and icing sugar together until pale and very soft. Add the vanilla. Sift in the cornflour and plain flour and mix to a soft dough. It will be quite soft; refrigerate for 15 minutes if it is too sticky to roll. Spread the desiccated coconut on a plate. Roll teaspoons of dough into balls and roll each in the coconut. Place on lined baking trays and press each ball lightly with a fork to flatten slightly.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Bake for 14 to 16 minutes. The biscuits should be just firm on the surface and very pale gold at the edges; the cornflour makes them look underdone longer than they are. Leave on the tray for 10 minutes before lifting; they are fragile when warm. Cool fully on a wire rack.
